sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=17, col=13).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 10, level 2.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 1 of about 38 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see the red markers:
1. Top red: center (17, 20), rows 16-19, cols 19-21
2. Middle red: center (26, 14), rows 25-28, cols 13-15
3. Bottom red: center (35, 14), rows 34-37, cols 13-15
And the green/N markers:
1. Top green: center (17, 39), rows 16-19, cols 38-40
2. Middle green: center (26, 48), rows 25-28, cols 47-49
3. Bottom green: center (35, 48), rows 34-37, cols 47-49
I clicked at (17, 13) which was not on any red marker. The top red marker is at center (17, 20). Let me try clicking at (17, 20).
But wait - looking at the layout, the red markers are positioned differently:
- Top red is at col 20 (right side of the left area)
- Middle red is at col 14 (left side)
- Bottom red is at col 14 (left side)
This is interesting - the top red marker is on the right side of its row's blocks, while the middle and bottom ones are on the left side. Let me look at the ASCII again to understand the layout.
Looking at the ASCII from before:
Row 17: cccccccccRRRcSScbbcppcYYcppcNNNccccccccccc
Row 26: ccRRRcwwcbbcbbcwwcSScppcggcSScggcggcNNNcc
Row 35: ccRRRcwwcSScbbcwwcbbcSScppcbbcggcSScNNNcc
So in row 17, the red marker (RRR) is at cols 11-13, and the blocks are to the right of it.
In row 26, the red marker (RRR) is at cols 3-5, and the blocks are to the right.
In row 35, the red marker (RRR) is at cols 3-5, and the blocks are to the right.
Wait, but the segmentation says the top red marker is at cols 19-21. Let me reconcile this. The ASCII I printed was from a cropped view. Let me look at the full row.
Actually, looking at the earlier output:
Row 17: NGGGGGGGGGGccccccccRRRcSScbbcppcYYcppcNNNcccccccccccGGGGGGGGGGGG
Row 26: NGGGGGGGGGGccRRRcwwcbbcbbcwwcSScppcggcSScggcggcNNNccGGGGGGGGGGGG
Row 35: NGGGGGGGGGGccRRRcwwcSScbbcwwcbbcSScppcbbcggcSScNNNccGGGGGGGGGGGG
So the red markers are at different column positions in the full ASCII. Let me count:
Row 17: "NGGGGGGGGGGccccccccRRRcSScbbcppcYYcppcNNNcccccccccccGGGGGGGGGGGG"
- N at col 0
- G at cols 1-10 (10 G's)
- c at cols 11-18 (8 c's)
- RRR at cols 19-21
- c at col 22
- SS at cols 23-24
- etc.
Row 26: "NGGGGGGGGGGccRRRcwwcbbcbbcwwcSScppcggcSScggcggcggcNNNccGGGGGGGGGGGG"
- N at col 0
- G at cols 1-10
- cc at cols 11-12
- RRR at cols 13-15
- c at col 16
- ww at cols 17-18
- etc.
So the red markers are at:
- Top: cols 19-21 (center ~20)
- Middle: cols 13-15 (center ~14)
- Bottom: cols 13-15 (center ~14)
This matches the segmentation! So the top red marker is further to the right than the middle and bottom ones.
Now, the blocks in each row:
Row 17: RRR at 19-21, then blocks at 23-24, 26-27, 29-30, 32-33, 35-36, then NNN at 38-40
Row 26: RRR at 13-15, then blocks at 17-18, 20-21, 23-24, 26-27, 29-30, 32-33, 35-36, 38-39, 41-42, 44-45, then NNN at 47-49
Row 35: RRR at 13-15, then blocks at 17-18, 20-21, 23-24, 26-27, 29-30, 32-33, 35-36, 38-39, 41-42, 44-45, then NNN at 47-49
So row 17 has 5 blocks (between RRR and NNN), while rows 26 and 35 have 10 blocks each.
Let me try clicking the top red marker at (17, 20).

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try clicking the top red marker at center (17, 20)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 17, 'col': 20}])
print("Board changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))

# Check row 17 after click
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print(f"Row 17: {ascii_lines[17][15:45]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
